query: effectiveness of verubecestat in treating Alzheimer’s disease

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Alzheimer disease is a progressive, chronic, neurodegenerative disease and the most common etiology of dementia. [1] In dementia patients aged 65 and older, Alzheimer disease accounts for two-thirds of the dementia cases, and it is the sixth most common cause of older adult mortality in the United States. [2] Alzheimer disease's characteristic hallmarks are extracellular neuritic plaques generated by the deposition of beta-amyloid protein and intracellular buildup of tau proteins, resulting in intracytoplasmic neurofibrillary tangles(NFT). [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyloid Beta Peptide -- Clinical Significance -- Teaching Points
retrieved_docs.content: Amyloid-β peptide is a key therapeutic target, with at least 2 FDA-approved drugs, lecanemab and donanemab, now available for early Alzheimer disease. [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: No drug-drug interactions are currently reported as of this writing. Conventional therapy for Alzheimer disease was used during pivotal trials, and aducanumab may be used concomitantly with the other medications utilized to manage AD. [7] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease Modifying Therapies of AD
retrieved_docs.content: New and specific disease-modifying therapies are being developed, and the FDA has recently approved some. For example, Aducanemab was approved by the FDA in June 2020. The FDA recently approved Lecanemab. Donanemab is also expected to receive FDA approval soon. These monoclonal antibodies act by removing amyloid from the brain as immunotherapy. Remternetug is another one of these promising amyloid-targeting immunotherapy drugs in the pipeline. [47] Aducanemab received accelerated FDA approval in June 2020. It was shown to reduce amyloid-beta plaque in the brain. However, it missed the primary phase III trial endpoint of clinical improvement. [48] Lecanemab received accelerated FDA approval in January 2023. It reduced the amyloid-beta burden in the brain. The phase III trial showed a 27% slowing of disease progression. [49] Donanemab is expected to receive FDA approval in 2023. It reduced the amyloid-beta burden in the brain and slowed cognitive decline by 35%. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The selectivity of aducanumab for aggregated β-amyloid forms results in reducing the brain burden of β-amyloid plaques . [17] [20] In a small subset of patients using tau PET, a reduction in hyper-phosphorylated tau was also observed in the cerebrospinal fluid and in medial temporal neurofibrillary tangles. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Shy-Drager Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The myeloperoxidase (MPO) inhibitor verdiperstat was shown to decrease microglial activation and neuronal rescue in a mouse model of MSA. A phase 3 placebo-controlled human trial on the efficacy of verdiperstat in improving the quality of life for patients with MSA is currently underway. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyloid Beta Peptide -- Clinical Significance -- Amyloid-β as a Therapeutic Target
retrieved_docs.content: Several clinical trials have investigated immunotherapy aimed at reducing amyloid plaque burden to slow disease progression. Most early trials failed to meet primary endpoints or were discontinued due to safety concerns. However, as of July 2024, the FDA has approved at least 2 anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies—lecanemab and donanemab—for early-stage Alzheimer disease. These agents reduce plaque burden on amyloid PET imaging and may offer modest benefits in delaying cognitive decline. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Galantamine -- Indications -- Non-FDA-approved uses of galantamine are listed below:
retrieved_docs.content: To maintain a good quality of sleep in patients with mild to moderate Alzheimer disease with or without vascular dementia [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease Modifying Therapies of AD
retrieved_docs.content: Amyloid Related Imaging Abnormalities (ARIA) [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease Modifying Therapies of AD
retrieved_docs.content: ARIA is an immune-mediated response to amyloid-targeting therapies in the cerebral vascular walls resulting in capillary leakage into perivascular spaces and hemorrhages of cortical and leptomeningeal arteries. There are 2 radiological types of ARIA: ARIA edema (ARIA-E) and ARIA hemorrhage (ARIA-H).

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Within the neuritic plaques that characterize Alzheimer disease neuropathologically, β-amyloid exists in several forms, eg, as monomers, oligomers, protofibrils, and fibrils. Oligomers and protofibrils are neurotoxic. Therefore, removal of them by monoclonal antibody therapy is thought to be helpful in the treatment of Alzheimer disease. Soderberg et al characterized the binding properties of aducanumab, gantenerumab, and lecanemab on several forms of amyloid-beta via immunodepletion, inhibition ELISA, and surface plasmon resonance studies. [16] Aducanumab, gantenerumab, and lecanemab bind to monomer amyloid-beta with low affinity. Lecanemab had 10-fold greater binding to amyloid protofibrils when compared to fibrils. Aducanumab and gantenerumab are bound preferably to β-amyloid fibrils over protofibrils. Soderberg et al concluded that these monoclonal antibody binding differences may be important for therapeutic efficacy and adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: In November 2020, the FDA's Peripheral and Central Nervous System Drugs Advisory Committee met to discuss the aducanumab BLA. A series of 4 votes culminated in the near-unanimous (there was a single abstention) verdict that the advisors did not find reasonable grounds to consider the results of the clinical trials as evidence of aducanumab's effectiveness for treating AD. Within days of the July 2021 accelerated approval decision, 3 members of the Advisory Committee, comprising one-third of its nongovernment members, resigned, citing "serious potential to impair future research" and deeming the FDA's ruling "the worst approval decision that FDA has made....." [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease Modifying Therapies of AD
retrieved_docs.content: In the past, the treatment of AD has primarily focused on managing the symptoms. By the time a patient is diagnosed with AD, the pathological process in the brain has been present for over a decade. However, with advancements in our understanding of the pathophysiology of AD and improvements in diagnostic testing with imaging and biochemical markers, we can now detect preclinical and presymptomatic stages of the disease, even in individuals with MCI.

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Approved Indication
retrieved_docs.content: Aducanumab is approved to treat Alzheimer disease in patients who have MCI-AD or who are in the mild dementia stage of the disease. [11] [12] [11] The first iteration of the package insert described the indication simply as "Alzheimer disease," but the indication was given greater specificity within days of the accelerated approval. [7] Aducanumab has not been studied to date in individuals without cognitive impairment or in those who have progressed beyond the mild stage of the disease. Aducanumab's continued use and full approval will require further verification of its clinical benefits in post-approval studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Symptomatic Therapies of AD
retrieved_docs.content: Traditionally, there has been no cure for AD, and symptomatic treatment remains the primary approach in everyday clinical practice. [40] [41] [42] Two categories of drugs are approved for treating AD: cholinesterase inhibitors and partial N-methyl D-aspartate (NMDA) antagonists.

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Dementia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: No pharmacologic approaches are FDA-approved explicitly for vascular dementia. Cholinesterase inhibitors, which increase the availability of acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft and may have a salubrious effect on cerebral blood flow, are often used in an off-label fashion in vascular dementia. In contrast, they form the backbone of pharmacotherapy in Alzheimer disease. These agents have a mild-to-moderate benefit of slowing the progression of cognitive decline in some patients, but they also have a significant adverse effect profile, including gastrointestinal distress, symptomatic bradycardia, sleep disturbances, and weight loss. Cholinesterase inhibitors are not specific to treating vascular dementia but are reasonable to consider as there is considerable overlap of mixed Alzheimer dementia with vascular dementia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent, meaning it mimics the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission. As a member of the parasympathomimetic class, galantamin exerts its therapeutic effects primarily through reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and allosteric modulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, actions that support cognitive function in conditions such as Alzheimer disease. Recognizing galantamin’s placement within the parasympathomimetic category helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, potential side‑effect profile, and appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 979
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs are classified as cholinesterasové inhibitory, reflecting their broader relationship to cholinesterase‑inhibiting agents. These drugs exert their therapeutic effect by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, thereby increasing acetylcholine availability in the central nervous system. Donepezil is a specific member of the anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drug class, exemplifying the use of acetylcholinesterase inhibition in the treatment of dementia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 788
retrieved_docs.content: The estrogen antagonist (antagonisté estrogenu) is directly related to estrogen (estrogeny) and functions as a modulator of estrogen activity. This antagonist is identified as centchroman and is classified within the broader group of hormone antagonists and related agents. While estrogen itself may be used to treat menopausal disorders, the presence of an estrogen antagonist such as centchroman highlights therapeutic strategies that can either block or modify estrogenic effects, which is important for managing symptoms and conditions associated with menopause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1582
retrieved_docs.content: Memantine is classified as a member of the ATC group “Other anti‑dementia drugs,” indicating that it is recognized within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical classification system as a medication used for the treatment of dementia, particularly Alzheimer’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2801
retrieved_docs.content: Diet therapy is recognized as an allowed therapeutic approach for several distinct conditions, including التهاب وريدي (venous inflammation), الخرف من نوع الزهايمر (Alzheimer’s‑type dementia), and كسر عنق عظم الفخذ (fracture of the femoral neck). In each case, incorporating appropriate dietary interventions can support overall management, aid recovery, and potentially improve clinical outcomes when used alongside other standard treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: sites (brain, CSF pathways, nerves), overlapping clinical entities (dementia, gait disorder, vascular headache), and common therapeutic strategies (extracranial ventricular shunting, neurosurgery, valproic acid), emphasizing the need for coordinated assessment and management of overlapping neurological disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2237
retrieved_docs.content: 1‑Methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin is classified as a dopaminergic agent, placing it within the broader pharmacologic class that modulates dopamine activity. This class of dopaminergic agents also includes the well‑known drug karbidopa, which is used to enhance central dopamine levels by inhibiting peripheral conversion of levodopa. Within the dopaminergic agent hierarchy, there is a distinct subgroup characterized by inhibitory dopamine reuptake mechanisms, referred to as “inhibitory vychytávání dopaminu.” By grouping 1‑methyl‑4‑fenyl‑1,2,3,6‑tetrahydropyridin, karbidopa, and the inhibitory‑reuptake subclass under the umbrella of dopaminergic agents, the summary clarifies how these compounds are related by their shared target of dopamine modulation and highlights their relevance for conditions such as Parkinson’s disease and other disorders where enhancing or regulating dopaminergic signaling is therapeutically important.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol and estrogeny are hormonal agents that may be used to treat menopausal disorders, providing therapeutic options for alleviating the symptoms associated with the menopausal transition. Both substances are forms of estrogen therapy and are considered in clinical management to improve quality of life for patients experiencing menopausal-related changes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑dementia drugs are classified as a subclass of psychoanaleptics, meaning they belong to the broader group of central‑nervous‑system stimulants that enhance mental activity. This classification reflects the pharmacologic action of anti‑dementia agents, which aim to improve cognition and counteract the decline associated with dementia by stimulating neural pathways, a property shared with other psychoanaleptic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium estrone sulfate is indicated as a possible treatment for menopausal disorders, while synthetic conjugated estrogens A and synthetic conjugated estrogens B are each identified as potential therapies for alleviating menopausal symptoms. These estrogenic agents represent therapeutic options that can be employed to manage the various clinical manifestations associated with menopause, providing clinicians with multiple pharmacologic choices to address both the broader disorder and its specific symptomatic presentations.

retrieved_docs.content: Karbamazepin is a therapeutic agent that may be used in the management of اضطراب الكرب التالي للرض, indicating its potential role in treating this specific disorder.

retrieved_docs.content: Late effects of cerebrovascular disease has parent اضطراب مخي وعائي, indicating that the long‑term sequelae of cerebrovascular disease are classified under the broader category of cerebrovascular disorder. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: Kolesevelam, a bile‑acid sequestrant employed to lower low‑density lipoprotein cholesterol and to improve glycemic control in type 2 diabetes, should not be prescribed to patients who have البَنكرِياس inflammation (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس), as the drug is contraindicated in the presence of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol, a potent form of estrogen, may be used to treat menopausal disorders, helping to alleviate symptoms such as hot flashes, vaginal dryness, and bone density loss that commonly occur during the menopausal transition.
